visits.text: Explore Akhaltsikhe, capital of the Samtskhe-Javakheti region and gateway to Georgia's remarkable southern attractions. The town's history reflects centuries of Georgian and Ottoman influence, creating a unique cultural blend visible in local architecture and traditions. Recently revitalized, the town center provides excellent facilities while maintaining authentic regional character.

Discover the magnificent Rabati Fortress, a stunning medieval castle complex completely restored in recent years to showcase its layered Ottoman and Georgian heritage. The fortress grounds encompass well-maintained gardens, a mosque, a church, and a synagogue, reflecting the region's multicultural history. Climbing the towers rewards visitors with spectacular views of the town and surrounding mountains.

activities.text: Wander through Rabati's illuminated grounds in the evening when atmospheric lighting transforms the fortress into a magical setting. The complex includes museums documenting local history and traditional crafts. Akhaltsikhe serves as the perfect base for exploring lesser-known destinations including Tmogvi, Khertvisi Fortress, and the remote valleys of southern Georgia.

days_plan.itin_title: Caucasus Mountain Odyssey: A Road Trip to Medieval Towers & Ancient Cave Cities

visits.text: Reach Batumi, Georgia's primary Black Sea resort city, known for its unique blend of historic architecture and striking contemporary buildings earning comparisons to Las Vegas. The beachfront promenade stretches for kilometers along pebbly shores backed by palm trees and modern sculptures. This cosmopolitan city pulses with energy during summer months when visitors from across the region flock to its seaside attractions.

Explore Batumi's charming Old Town, where European-style buildings, narrow lanes, and the historic Orta Jame Mosque reflect the city's multicultural heritage. The region of Adjara maintains distinct cultural traditions with approximately 30 percent Muslim population, distinguishing it from other Georgian regions. Musical fountains, cycling paths, and vibrant nightlife create an atmosphere of celebration.

activities.text: Hike through the Batumi Botanical Garden, a steep but rewarding trail ascending hillsides covered with exotic plant collections culminating in spectacular Black Sea panoramas. The extensive grounds showcase flora from around the world in a stunning coastal setting. Evening brings the famous dancing fountains to life while the illuminated Boulevard invites leisurely waterfront strolls.

days_plan.itin_title: Caucasus Mountain Odyssey: A Road Trip to Medieval Towers & Ancient Cave Cities

visits.text: Arrive in Borjomi, Georgia's famous spa resort town nestled in a forested gorge and renowned worldwide for its naturally carbonated mineral water. The town developed as a fashionable retreat during the 19th century when the Russian imperial family built their summer residence here. Dense pine forests, therapeutic springs, and crisp mountain air continue to attract visitors seeking relaxation and natural wellness.

Stroll through Borjomi Central Park where visitors can sample the famous mineral water directly from the source at no charge. A vintage cable car ascends from the park to panoramic viewpoints overlooking the town and surrounding peaks. The historic bathhouse buildings showcase elegant 19th-century architecture reflecting Borjomi's golden age as a therapeutic destination.

activities.text: Visit the ornate Romanov Palace, the summer residence of the Russian imperial family featuring distinctive striped red-and-yellow stonework and romantic gardens. The palace grounds offer peaceful walking paths through manicured landscapes. Consider exploring portions of the vast Borjomi-Kharagauli National Park, one of Europe's largest protected areas featuring diverse ecosystems and extensive hiking trails.

days_plan.itin_title: Caucasus Mountain Odyssey: A Road Trip to Medieval Towers & Ancient Cave Cities

visits.text: Discover Chiatura, a fascinating industrial city known for its remarkable network of Soviet-era cable cars connecting the valley floor to surrounding hillsides. Established in the 1870s following the discovery of manganese deposits, the town once supplied 60 percent of the world's manganese ore. The aerial ropeways, commissioned by Stalin himself, continue serving residents as daily transportation.

Ride the distinctive cable cars, nicknamed 'flying coffins' for their weathered appearance, offering spectacular views of the valley and colorful apartment blocks clinging to steep hillsides. The brutalist cable car station buildings retain original Soviet insignia including hammer-and-sickle symbols. Fans of industrial heritage and Soviet architecture find Chiatura an absolute treasure trove.

activities.text: Stop at Katskhi Pillar, an extraordinary natural limestone column rising 40 meters from the valley floor with a small chapel perched improbably on top. A solitary monk inhabits this vertiginous hermitage, descending a daunting ladder twice weekly for supplies. Dating possibly from the 6th century, this unique monument exemplifies the remarkable lengths Georgian monks pursued for spiritual isolation.

days_plan.itin_title: Caucasus Mountain Odyssey: A Road Trip to Medieval Towers & Ancient Cave Cities

visits.text: Journey to Davit Gareja, an extraordinary 6th-century monastery complex carved into semi-desert hillsides near the Azerbaijani border. Founded by one of the Thirteen Syrian Fathers who spread Christianity throughout the Caucasus, this remote pilgrimage site comprises hundreds of caves, cells, and chapels decorated with medieval frescoes. The stark landscape creates an atmosphere of profound isolation and spiritual contemplation.

Climb to the upper monastery complex for views across the painted desert landscape stretching to the Azerbaijan frontier. Ancient cave cells containing faded but still visible religious paintings dot the cliffsides, testimony to centuries of monastic devotion in this austere environment. Active monks continue to inhabit portions of this remarkable heritage site.

activities.text: Explore the otherworldly semi-arid terrain surrounding Davit Gareja, characterized by undulating hills, sparse vegetation, and vivid earth tones rarely seen elsewhere in Georgia. This unique ecosystem supports distinctive flora and fauna adapted to the dry conditions. The journey here from Sighnaghi passes through varied landscapes demonstrating the country's remarkable geographic diversity.

days_plan.itin_title: Caucasus Mountain Odyssey: A Road Trip to Medieval Towers & Ancient Cave Cities

visits.text: Explore Gori, the birthplace of Joseph Stalin and home to the controversial museum documenting his life and legacy. The Stalin Museum complex includes his childhood home preserved under a protective pavilion, his personal railway carriage, and extensive exhibitions of photographs and artifacts. Visitors can examine this complicated historical figure while walking through streets still bearing Soviet-era architecture.

Climb to Gori Fortress, a medieval citadel crowning a rocky hill at the heart of the city with commanding views of the surrounding plains. Archaeological evidence suggests fortifications existed here since before the 1st century BC, making this site witness to millennia of Georgian history. The restored walls and towers provide excellent vantage points for understanding the region's strategic importance.

activities.text: Visit the Sergi Makalatia Historical and Ethnographic Museum to gain deeper understanding of Georgian cultural traditions and regional history beyond the Stalin narrative. The Cathedral of the Nativity of the Blessed Virgin offers peaceful gardens for contemplation. Gori's central location makes it an ideal base for exploring multiple nearby attractions.

days_plan.itin_title: Caucasus Mountain Odyssey: A Road Trip to Medieval Towers & Ancient Cave Cities

visits.text: Travel the legendary Georgian Military Highway, one of the most spectacular mountain roads in the world connecting Tbilisi to the high Caucasus. This historic route winds through dramatic gorges, past ancient fortresses, and along rushing rivers that have shaped the landscape for millennia. Every curve reveals new vistas of snow-capped peaks and verdant valleys.

Stop at the Russia-Georgia Friendship Monument, a striking Soviet-era mosaic panorama built in 1983 to commemorate bilateral relations. The colorful semi-circular structure features intricate tile work depicting scenes from Georgian and Russian history. More importantly, the viewing platform offers spectacular panoramas of the surrounding mountain wilderness.

activities.text: Discover Gudauri, Georgia's premier mountain resort featuring 35 kilometers of ski slopes served by 15 modern lifts during winter months. In summer, the area transforms into a paradise for hikers and paragliders seeking adventure amid pristine alpine landscapes. The resort sits at an elevation of 2,200 meters, offering refreshing mountain air and dramatic scenery year-round.

days_plan.itin_title: Caucasus Mountain Odyssey: A Road Trip to Medieval Towers & Ancient Cave Cities

visits.text: Venture inland from Batumi to discover the mountainous heart of Adjara, a region of forested peaks and traditional villages rarely visited by international travelers. The Lesser Caucasus mountains rise dramatically from the coastal plain, creating landscapes of exceptional beauty. This off-the-beaten-path territory rewards adventurous visitors seeking authentic Georgian mountain culture.

Experience the remarkable Khulo cable car, a small red gondola soaring 350 meters above a deep valley to connect the villages of Khulo and Tago. This spectacular ride offers bird's-eye views of precipitous terrain and traditional highland settlements. The villages themselves provide glimpses of unhurried mountain life far removed from coastal tourist development.

activities.text: Explore hiking opportunities throughout mountainous Adjara, ranging from day walks to multi-day wilderness treks through pristine highland environments. Numerous guesthouses in remote villages offer authentic hospitality and home-cooked regional specialties. The area remains genuinely undeveloped, providing adventurous travelers with experiences increasingly difficult to find elsewhere in Georgia.

days_plan.itin_title: Caucasus Mountain Odyssey: A Road Trip to Medieval Towers & Ancient Cave Cities

visits.text: Arrive in Kutaisi, Georgia's third-largest city and former capital, maintaining an old-world charm that distinguishes it from the more cosmopolitan Tbilisi. Situated along the banks of the Rioni River, the city has served as an important cultural center for millennia. The elegant Lado Meskhishvili State Drama Theatre and surrounding architecture reflect the city's distinguished heritage.

Visit Bagrati Cathedral, a UNESCO World Heritage masterpiece originally constructed in the 11th century as a symbol of medieval Georgian ecclesiastical architecture. Though partially destroyed by Ottoman forces in the 17th century, restoration efforts have returned the cathedral to its former glory. The hilltop location provides commanding views over the city and river below.

activities.text: Explore Gelati Monastery, another UNESCO site founded by King David the Builder in 1106 as an academy that became the medieval world's preeminent center of Georgian learning. The monastery complex includes remarkable frescoes and mosaics representing the pinnacle of medieval Georgian artistic achievement. Browse the vibrant Green Bazaar where vendors display fresh produce, spices, and local delicacies.

days_plan.itin_title: Caucasus Mountain Odyssey: A Road Trip to Medieval Towers & Ancient Cave Cities

visits.text: The Khareba Wine Tunnel is 7.7 km of tunnels drilled into the Caucasus rock, opened in 1962 - the year the World Congress of Vine and Wine was held in Georgia. The Soviet engineers built it as a military storage facility; it was converted into a wine cellar after independence and acquired by Winery Khareba in 2006. Temperature inside: a constant 12-14°C year-round, which makes it among the most stable natural cellars anywhere. Currently 30,000+ bottles age here, the oldest over 20 years. Visitors access only the tourist section of the tunnel, but even that stretch gives a clear sense of scale. Guided tour + tasting: from 10 GEL. Touristique? Yes. Worth it? Also yes.

Gremi Fortress, on the road from Telavi to Kvareli, is a 16th-century church and tower complex built by King Levan of Kakheti in 1565. The tower stands on a rocky outcrop above the Alazani plain and houses the Church of the Archangels, with frescoes still intact. In 1615, Shah Abbas I of Persia destroyed the town of Gremi - only the church fortress survived. It's on the UNESCO tentative list and draws almost no crowds. Stop for 45 minutes on the way from Telavi.

Nekresi Monastery clings to a forested hillside above the Alazani valley, reachable by shuttle jeep from the base (the road is too rough for regular cars). Founded in the 4th century, repeatedly rebuilt through the 8th century, the complex includes a small fire-worshippers' temple at the valley floor - evidence that Zoroastrianism preceded Christianity in this part of Kakheti. The view over the valley from the monastery terrace, particularly in October when the forest turns, is among the best in the region.

activities.text: Tasting at Kindzmarauli Marani. The Kindzmarauli is a red semi-sweet AOC wine, made exclusively from Saperavi grown in five villages of the Kvareli municipality. Stalin, who was Georgian by birth, had crates shipped to Roosevelt and Churchill during the Second World War. Today it is Georgia's most exported wine style. The factory tour is not glamorous, but the qvevri scale is spectacular and the tasting explains the unusual vinification method - fermentation is stopped by cold to preserve natural residual sugar. 25-40€.

Tsinandali Estate, 10 minutes from Kvareli, is the former property of Prince Alexandre Chavchavadze (1786-1846), the poet who first introduced European winemaking techniques to Georgia in the 19th century. The restored manor house and 18-hectare botanical garden are open for visits. The Tsinandali white (Rkatsiteli-Mtsvane blend, European method rather than qvevri) has been produced here since 1886, making it one of the oldest continuously labelled Georgian wines.

days_plan.itin_title: Georgia: 7 Days at the Birthplace of Wine - Tbilisi, Kakheti and the Qvevri Route

visits.text: Experience Martvili Canyon, a stunning natural wonder where emerald-green waters flow through a narrow limestone gorge draped in moss and lush vegetation. Short boat rides carry visitors through the most scenic sections, gliding beneath overhanging cliffs and past cascading waterfalls. The canyon's intimate scale creates an atmosphere of peaceful immersion in pristine natural beauty.

Explore the broader Martvili area, home to numerous waterfalls, canyons, and natural attractions that remain largely undiscovered by international tourists. Visit Martvili Monastery, the Kaghu Waterfall, Balda Canyon, and Oghueshi Waterfall for a comprehensive appreciation of the region's diverse landscapes. The area's rich history complements its natural wonders.

activities.text: Visit Okatse Canyon, featuring a dramatic hanging walkway suspended above a gorge ranging from 35 to 50 meters deep. The canyon stretches 16 kilometers though visitors can walk approximately 1.5 kilometers along the metal pathway. Breathtaking views of the chasm below create exhilarating sensations for those comfortable with heights.

days_plan.itin_title: Caucasus Mountain Odyssey: A Road Trip to Medieval Towers & Ancient Cave Cities

visits.text: Arrive in Mestia, the capital of Svaneti and gateway to Georgia's most dramatic high-mountain landscapes surrounded by peaks exceeding 5,000 meters. This remote region developed unique cultural traditions, language, and architecture due to centuries of isolation in deep valleys. The distinctive Svanetian defensive towers, built between the 9th and 12th centuries, punctuate the landscape as symbols of this fiercely independent community.

Visit the Svaneti Museum of History and Ethnography to understand the remarkable heritage of the Svan people, including their distinctive religious practices, crafts, and social structures. The museum houses important medieval icons and artifacts preserved in these remote valleys when lowland Georgia faced repeated invasions. Traditional stone tower houses continue functioning as family residences throughout the region.

activities.text: Hike to Koruldi Lakes for spectacular panoramic views of Svaneti's glacier-capped peaks and alpine meadows carpeted with wildflowers during summer months. The Chalaadi Glacier trail offers another accessible day hike approaching ancient ice formations. Two ski resorts, Tetnuldi and Hatsvali, provide winter sports opportunities while backcountry skiing attracts adventurous visitors seeking untouched powder.

days_plan.itin_title: Caucasus Mountain Odyssey: A Road Trip to Medieval Towers & Ancient Cave Cities

visits.text: Svetitskhoveli Cathedral dominates the centre of Mtskheta and has served as the spiritual and coronation seat of Georgian kings since it was completed by architect Arsukisdze in 1010-1029. At 54 metres high, it was the tallest building in Georgia until 2004. The name means 'life-giving pillar', a reference to the column said to have grown from the spot where Christ's robe was buried after being brought from Jerusalem in the 1st century by a Mtskhetan Jew. The story sounds like allegory; the cathedral built around it is entirely real. UNESCO World Heritage Site since 1994. Free entry; open 8 AM-8 PM.

Jvari Monastery perches on the cliff above the confluence of the Kura and Aragvi rivers, exactly where Saint Nino planted a wooden cross in the 4th century after converting the Georgian kingdom to Christianity. The current domed church dates to the late 6th century - one of the earliest intact examples of Georgian ecclesiastical architecture. Mikhail Lermontov set the opening of his poem 'Mtsyri' here. Drive up rather than walk; the road is steep and cars park at the monastery gate.

Samtavro Monastery (a short walk north of Svetitskhoveli) holds the grave of King Mirian III, the first Christian monarch of Georgia, and his queen Nana - both canonised saints. The small 4th-century domed church is the oldest surviving part of the complex. Unusually for a Georgian monastery, it is administered by nuns rather than monks, and the interior is always lit by candles rather than electric light.

activities.text: Lunch at Salobie restaurant in Mtskheta, a long-standing local address known for its lobiani (bean-filled flatbread), traditional khachapuri, and grilled meats. Tourist-adjacent but legitimately good; the lobiani here is better than most versions you will find in Tbilisi. Budget 15-25€ per person.

Wine souvenir stop before the airport: the best picks to carry home in your suitcase are a Pheasant's Tears Rkatsiteli or Tavkveri (~25-40€ at the estate, if you bought direct), an Alaverdi Monastery Cellar Saperavi (~35€), and a Schuchmann Vinoterra Kisi Qvevri. EU customs allows 6 bottles in hold luggage duty-free; pack them in the padded wine carriers most Kakheti estates sell at the door. The Tbilisi airport duty-free has a decent but overpriced Georgian selection as a backup.

days_plan.itin_title: Caucasus Mountain Odyssey: A Road Trip to Medieval Towers & Ancient Cave Cities
Georgia: 7 Days at the Birthplace of Wine - Tbilisi, Kakheti and the Qvevri Route

visits.text: Sighnaghi sits on a ridge at 800 metres with the Alazani valley below and the snow-capped Greater Caucasus behind - a view that justifies the drive entirely. King Erekle II built the 4 km of fortified walls and 23 towers in the 18th century to protect the town from Persian raids. Today about 2,000 people live here and the whole centre has been carefully (some say over-carefully) restored. One genuinely odd detail: since 2007, the town has operated Georgia's only 24/7 civil registry office, allowing couples to marry without an appointment at any hour of the night. The scheme to attract tourists worked.

Bodbe Monastery, 2 km south of Sighnaghi, is where Saint Nino - the 4th-century woman credited with converting Georgia to Christianity - is buried. She arrived from Cappadocia around 330 AD, convinced Queen Nana and eventually King Mirian III to adopt the faith. The monastery's church, rebuilt in the 9th-11th centuries, holds her tomb and a constant flow of pilgrims who make the steep descent to the spring below the grounds, said to heal the sick. Not touristic in the way Sighnaghi is - still a working pilgrimage site.

Pheasant's Tears winery, right in Sighnaghi's centre, is the address that put Georgian natural wine on the global map. Painter John Wurdeman settled in Georgia in the 1990s, learned the qvevri method, and in 2007 launched a biodynamic estate working exclusively with nearly-extinct indigenous varieties: Tavkveri, Aladasturi, Chitistvala, Tsolikouri. The wines are unlike anything produced elsewhere. The attached restaurant serves traditional Kakhetian food paired to the estate's range. Tasting of 6 wines: approximately 25-40€. Reservation required.

activities.text: Walk the full circuit of the Sighnaghi ramparts. The path is mostly unguarded and unrailed - which sounds alarming but means you can walk the 4 km of walls at your own pace with views west over the Alazani valley on one side and the town's terracotta rooftops on the other. Allow 1.5 hours; the walls are uneven stone so wear proper shoes.

Evening supra experience at the Pheasant's Tears restaurant or at a local guesthouse. A supra is not a dinner - it is a ritual with a tamada (toastmaster) who conducts a precise sequence of toasts: to the homeland, to ancestors, to women, to children, to peace. Do not drink between toasts. Do not refuse a toast without good reason. This is exactly the kind of thing that sounds awkward in description and becomes one of the clearest memories of the trip.

days_plan.itin_title: Caucasus Mountain Odyssey: A Road Trip to Medieval Towers & Ancient Cave Cities
Georgia: 7 Days at the Birthplace of Wine - Tbilisi, Kakheti and the Qvevri Route
Georgia: 8,000 Years of Wine in 7 Days

visits.text: Reach Stepantsminda (formerly Kazbegi), the gateway to some of the most spectacular mountain scenery in the entire Caucasus range. This charming highland town sits at 1,740 meters elevation in the shadow of Mount Kazbek, a dormant volcano rising to 5,054 meters that dominates the horizon. The region offers exceptional opportunities for hiking, horseback riding, and experiencing traditional mountain village life.

Hike to Gergeti Trinity Church (Tsminda Sameba), Georgia's most iconic landmark perched on a hilltop at 2,170 meters with Mount Kazbek as its backdrop. This 14th-century stone sanctuary appears to float among the clouds, creating one of the most photographed scenes in the Caucasus. The moderately challenging trail rewards visitors with increasingly dramatic views as elevation increases.

activities.text: Explore the Truso Valley, a remote glacial landscape dotted with mineral springs, travertine formations, and abandoned stone villages. The valley stretches toward the Russian border through terrain of otherworldly beauty, featuring multicolored rock formations and natural carbonated springs. This off-the-beaten-path adventure showcases the raw wilderness that makes Georgia's mountains so exceptional.

days_plan.itin_title: Caucasus Mountain Odyssey: A Road Trip to Medieval Towers & Ancient Cave Cities

visits.text: The Old Town sets the tone immediately: cobblestone alleys, carved wooden balconies jutting over the street, and the ruins of Narikala Fortress draped across the ridge above. The fortress dates from the 4th century, expanded by Arab emirs in the 8th century and by the Safavids after that - each conqueror added a layer, and the result is a patchwork of stone you can wander for an hour without retracing your steps. The cable car from the Bridge of Peace costs 6 GEL return and saves a sweaty climb. Go late afternoon for the light.

The Sameba Cathedral - completed in 2004 after 10 years of construction - is the largest Orthodox cathedral in the Caucasus at 84 metres high, visible from most of the city. The interior is deliberately austere: dark stone, gold iconostasis, the low hum of monks chanting if you arrive at the right hour. Controversial when built (the site displaced an old Armenian cemetery), it's since become a genuine focal point of Georgian religious life. No admission fee; modest dress required.

The Bridge of Peace - Michele de Lucchi's steel-and-glass pedestrian bridge, opened 2010 - cuts a deliberately jarring silhouette against the medieval skyline. Locals were divided at first; most have made peace with it. Walk it at dusk when the LED canopy starts to glow and the fortress reflects in the Mtkvari below. It costs nothing and takes five minutes, but the view both ways earns its place in the itinerary.

The Georgian National Museum on Rustaveli Avenue holds the two things you came to see. First, the Gold Treasury: Scythian goldwork from the 3rd-2nd centuries BC, buried in royal kurgans in the Alazani valley - the craftsmanship is finer than most European goldsmiths could manage a thousand years later. Second, the archaeological hall with the Neolithic qvevris from Shulaveris Gora, dated to around 6000 BC. These are the jars that proved the 8,000-year claim - small, unadorned, with tartaric acid residue that changed the history of wine. They sit in a glass case with very little drama. They deserve more. Budget 2 hours; entry 7 GEL.

Abanotubani - the Bath Quarter - is where Tbilisi began. The legend: King Vakhtang Gorgasali was hunting in 458 AD when a pheasant he shot fell into a hot spring and cooked. He founded a city around the source, naming it Tbili ('warm' in Georgian). The domed brick bathhouses visible today date from the 17th-18th centuries. Pouchkine wrote from here that he had 'never seen anything more luxurious than the baths of Tiflis.' The sulfur smell is real and immediate - part of the experience.

The Mtatsminda funicular climbs to 727 metres above the city - the original cable was installed in 1905, rebuilt several times, and still runs. At the top: a Soviet-era amusement park sits alongside a panoramic restaurant and a small church. The view over Tbilisi at dusk is the best vantage point in the city, the entire bowl of the valley, the fortress, the two cathedrals, and the towers of the new financial district all in one frame. Funicular ticket: 3 GEL each way.

The Dry Bridge flea market spreads along the Mtkvari embankment daily, best on weekends: Soviet memorabilia, icons, old cameras, porcelain, military medals, handmade jewellery, and - if you know what you're looking at - antique hand-carved qvevri lids and old wine vessels. The sellers are mostly pensioners supplementing their income; haggling is expected but not aggressive. It's one of those places where you understand a city's 20th century through its cast-off objects.

Rustaveli Avenue is Tbilisi's central spine - 1.5 kilometres of 19th-century neoclassical buildings housing the National Museum, Parliament, Opera, and Rustaveli Theatre. Built during the Russian imperial period to signal that Tbilisi was a proper European capital, it takes itself seriously in the right way. Walk it from Freedom Square toward Rustaveli metro; the architectural ambition reads best from the middle of the road, not the pavement.

Mtskheta is 30 minutes north of Tbilisi and several centuries removed in atmosphere. Georgia's ancient capital - seat of the Iberian kingdom from the 3rd century BC to the 5th century AD - sits at the confluence of the Mtkvari and Aragvi rivers. UNESCO listed it in 1994. The Svetitskhoveli Cathedral (completed 1029) is the spiritual centre of Georgian Orthodoxy; tradition holds that the robe of Christ is buried beneath the pillar at the crossing. Whether or not that's true, the pilgrimage energy in the nave is unmistakable - the cathedral is almost never empty.

Jvari Monastery crowns the hill directly above Mtskheta - the view from its terrace of the two rivers meeting is one of those compositions that explains, without any commentary, why someone chose to build here in the 6th century. The monastery is small (one church, a courtyard, a handful of monks), but the location is exact: Jvari means 'cross' in Georgian, and Nino is said to have erected a wooden cross on this hill in 327 AD when she converted the kingdom. The current stone building dates from 586-605 AD.

activities.text: First wine of the trip at 8000 Vintages on Erekle II Street: over 200 Georgian references by the glass and bottle, rotated by region and style. Ask for the flight comparing Rkatsiteli in qvevri versus stainless steel - the same grape, the same valley, two completely different wines. It's the clearest possible demonstration of why the vessel matters as much as the variety. The staff speak English well and genuinely enjoy the explaining.

Dinner at Shavi Lomi ('The Black Lion') in the Baths quarter: Georgian cooking with a modern hand, served in the ground floor of an old townhouse. Order the khinkali (Georgian dumplings, meat-filled, eaten by hand - never cut them or the juice escapes), the Adjarian khachapuri (boat-shaped, with a runny egg and knob of butter in the centre), and a glass of Saperavi from qvevri to close. The whole meal with wine comes to 35-50 GEL per person. Tbilisi's quality-to-price ratio is one of the most remarkable in Europe.

An hour in the sulfur baths needs no justification. Chreli-Abano (the one with the tiled blue facade) and the Royal Orbeliani Baths both offer private rooms: hot sulfur water, a scrub-down from a bath attendant (the kisi massage), and complete silence except for water echoing off old stone. The basic soak runs 30 GEL/hour for a private room; add the massage for 60 GEL. Go midday - mornings fill with families, evenings with tourists.

The Fabrika complex - a Soviet textile factory converted in 2017 into a cultural hub - is what happens when a city decides to remember its past without being nostalgic about it. The factory floor is now a courtyard with container bars, wine shops, and art studios. The Naturalist Wine Bar inside specialises in qvevri-only producers, many making a few thousand bottles a year. It's an evening place: grab a glass of Chinuri pétillant naturel and stay in the courtyard until you lose track of time.

The farewell dinner belongs at Barbarestan. Chef David Lomadze built the menu around a recipe book written by Duchess Barbara Jorjadze in 1873 - every dish traces back to a specific historical recipe, updated for modern kitchens. The result is Georgian cuisine as it was before the Soviet period standardised everything: more herb-driven, more layered, less predictable. The walnut preparations alone span six dishes. Reserve a week ahead; 50-80 USD per person with wine. If you splurge once on this trip, this is the meal.

Last wine stop: the Naturalist Wine Bar in the Fabrika courtyard. This is where Tbilisi's young wine crowd goes - producers you won't find in the tourist districts, pét-nats from tiny farms in Kartli, experimental qvevri-concrete hybrids from the new generation. Order something you can't pronounce and ask the bartender where it's from. That conversation, at the end of seven days, will land differently than it would have at the beginning.

Lunch at Salobie in Mtskheta town: a sprawling traditional restaurant under vine trellises, best-known for its lobiani - flatbread stuffed with spiced kidney beans. Order it with the walnut-herb salad and a final glass of Saperavi. It's a simple meal in a simple setting. After seven days of winery tastings and monastery cellars, that simplicity is exactly right. Arrive by 12h30 before the Tbilisi tour groups fill every table.

days_plan.itin_title: Caucasus Mountain Odyssey: A Road Trip to Medieval Towers & Ancient Cave Cities
Georgia: 7 Days at the Birthplace of Wine - Tbilisi, Kakheti and the Qvevri Route
Georgia: 7 Days at the Birthplace of Wine - Tbilisi, Kakheti and the Qvevri Route
Georgia: 7 Days at the Birthplace of Wine - Tbilisi, Kakheti and the Qvevri Route
Georgia: 8,000 Years of Wine in 7 Days
Georgia: 8,000 Years of Wine in 7 Days
Georgia: 8,000 Years of Wine in 7 Days
Georgia: 8,000 Years of Wine in 7 Days

visits.text: Alaverdi Cathedral is 50 metres high and was completed between 1010 and 1029 - the tallest medieval church in Georgia. But the building is almost secondary to what happens in the basement: monks here have been making wine without interruption since 1011. During the Soviet era, when private winemaking was technically forbidden, the monastery continued in what the brothers describe with considerable understatement as 'a spirit of quiet persistence.' The marani below the cathedral today holds approximately 100 indigenous varieties, some resurrected from as few as five surviving plants. Twelve centuries of unbroken viticulture, under one roof.

Telavi is Kakheti's administrative capital - 20,000 people, a weekly market that's been running for centuries, and a cluster of wine estates within 20 minutes in every direction. The old quarter around the 17th-century Batonis-tsikhe fortress is compact and walkable. The 900-year-old plane tree in the town centre has a circumference of 11 metres. It was already old when Alaverdi was new.

The Khareba tunnel in Kvareli started as a Soviet military project in 1962 - 7.7 kilometres bored into the mountain to shelter command infrastructure in the event of nuclear war. It was never used for that. Converted into a wine cellar in 2003, it now holds over 25,000 bottles ageing at a constant 14°C, which the rock maintains automatically. You travel the tunnel by electric cart, stopping at intervals for tastings. It's unapologetically touristy, and the sheer scale of the thing - standing at the far end of a wine-filled military corridor, watching your breath fog in the cold - is genuinely strange and worth the trip.

Tsinandali Estate was the property of Prince Alexandre Chavchavadze (1786-1846), the poet who first introduced European winemaking techniques to Georgia in the 19th century. The manor and its 18-hectare botanical garden have been beautifully restored. The Tsinandali white - a Rkatsiteli-Mtsvane blend, no qvevri, European-style ageing - was first produced in 1886, making it one of the oldest continuously produced wine labels in the Caucasus. Entry 5-8 GEL; worth an hour.

activities.text: Schuchmann Wines in Kisiskhevi (15 minutes from Telavi) represents the other face of Georgian viticulture: German investment, 165 working qvevris, a modern cellar, and a gastronomic restaurant. Burkhard Schuchmann arrived in 2008 and built what amounts to a Burgundy grand cru estate - with qvevris instead of barrels. The Vinoterra Rkatsiteli Qvevri is the benchmark amber wine for skeptics: structured, tannic, nothing like white wine as commonly understood, completely persuasive. The tasting covers both qvevri and classical method wines - a useful comparison for understanding what the vessel actually does to a wine.

An evening supra - a Georgian feast - is not a dinner. It's a ritual with a tamada (the toast-master) who leads toasts at prescribed intervals: to the homeland, to the ancestors, to the women, to the children, to peace, to the guest. You don't drink between toasts; you wait. The toasts get longer and more elaborate as the evening progresses. If you can no longer drink, say so clearly in advance - refusing mid-toast is the only real faux pas. Ask your guesthouse to organise a family supra. The experience is irreplaceable and costs almost nothing.

The Kindzmarauli tasting at Kindzmarauli Marani is the most historically loaded pour of the trip. Kindzmarauli is a protected AOC semi-sweet red - Saperavi grapes grown only in five villages around Kvareli, fermentation stopped by cold to retain natural residual sugar. Stalin, born 150 km west in Gori, was devoted to it: he shipped cases to Roosevelt and Churchill during the Second World War, and it became the most exported Georgian wine of the Soviet era. The reserve aged in qvevri is the version to judge by; the export classic is a different wine entirely.

Kvareli town itself is quieter and less groomed than Sighnaghi - more working village than destination. The morning market on the main street: dried churchkhela in long strings, walnuts by the kilo, jars of tkemali (sour plum sauce), and wine sold by locals from unlabelled plastic bottles that are sometimes the best thing you'll drink all week. Bring cash - the market operates entirely in GEL.

days_plan.itin_title: Georgia: 7 Days at the Birthplace of Wine - Tbilisi, Kakheti and the Qvevri Route
Georgia: 8,000 Years of Wine in 7 Days
Georgia: 8,000 Years of Wine in 7 Days

visits.text: Explore Tskaltubo, a once-glamorous Soviet spa resort where mineral waters have attracted visitors since the 13th century. During the USSR era, more than 20 sanatoriums and 9 bathhouses served thousands of guests annually, including Stalin who maintained a private cottage here. The town's grandeur faded following Georgian independence and civil war, leaving hauntingly beautiful abandoned buildings.

Wander through the atmospheric ruins of Soviet-era sanatoriums, their neoclassical facades and ornate interiors slowly being reclaimed by nature. These structures offer remarkable insights into Soviet architectural ambitions and the era's approach to public health and recreation. A few facilities have reopened, allowing visitors to experience the therapeutic mineral waters that made Tskaltubo famous.

activities.text: Visit Prometheus Cave (Kumistavi Cave), one of Georgia's most spectacular natural attractions featuring illuminated stalactites, stalagmites, underground rivers, and vast cavern chambers. The cave system extends over a kilometer with walkways and atmospheric lighting creating a magical underground journey. Boat rides through the cave's lower levels add another dimension to this subterranean adventure.

days_plan.itin_title: Caucasus Mountain Odyssey: A Road Trip to Medieval Towers & Ancient Cave Cities

visits.text: Discover Uplistsikhe, one of the oldest urban settlements in the Caucasus, carved entirely from rock on a plateau above the Mtkvari River. Dating from the late Bronze Age and flourishing as a major trading hub in the 6th century BC, this remarkable cave city once served as a vital stop on the Silk Road connecting Asia to Europe. Hundreds of caves, chambers, and tunnels honeycomb the sandstone formations.

Explore the ancient cave city's impressive infrastructure including a large central square, the Queen Tamar Hall, wine cellars carved into rock, a pagan temple, and a medieval Christian church. An ancient secret tunnel once provided escape routes during sieges, while carved channels distributed water throughout the settlement. Artifacts discovered here are now displayed in Tbilisi's National Museum.

activities.text: Walk through the Uplistsikhe archaeological complex, imagining the bustling market town that once thrived in these stone chambers. The 10th-century Uplistsuli Church dominates the site's highest point, its simple stone construction contrasting with the pre-Christian caves surrounding it. Sunset casts golden light across the rock formations, creating magical photographic opportunities.

days_plan.itin_title: Caucasus Mountain Odyssey: A Road Trip to Medieval Towers & Ancient Cave Cities

visits.text: Journey to Ushguli, a UNESCO World Heritage community considered the highest continuously inhabited settlement in Europe at approximately 2,400 meters elevation. This tiny village of around 200 residents preserves medieval defensive towers dating from the 9th century in a setting of extraordinary alpine beauty. The challenging 4x4 road from Mestia takes roughly 3 hours, crossing wild mountain terrain.

Explore the cluster of ancient hamlets comprising Ushguli, where traditional stone architecture remains virtually unchanged for centuries. Mighty Mount Shkhara, Georgia's highest peak at 5,193 meters, dominates the horizon with its glaciated slopes. The remote location, accessible only during summer months, preserves an atmosphere of timeless isolation increasingly rare in the modern world.

activities.text: Arrange mountain trekking or horseback riding excursions departing from Ushguli into the surrounding wilderness. The multi-day Mestia to Ushguli trek ranks among Georgia's finest hiking experiences, traversing high passes and traditional villages over 4 days. Simple guesthouses provide authentic accommodation where visitors share meals with local families maintaining centuries-old traditions.

days_plan.itin_title: Caucasus Mountain Odyssey: A Road Trip to Medieval Towers & Ancient Cave Cities

visits.text: Marvel at Vardzia, an extraordinary cave monastery city excavated from sheer cliffsides during Georgia's 12th-century golden age under Queen Tamar. This remarkable complex stretches approximately 500 meters along the rock face and rises 19 levels high, originally containing over 6,000 apartments, churches, and functional spaces. The site has been submitted for UNESCO World Heritage consideration.

Explore more than 400 accessible rooms including 12 chapels, 25 wine cellars, a bakery, pharmacies, and an intricate tunnel system connecting different sections. The Church of the Dormition preserves remarkable frescoes including a famous portrait of Queen Tamar herself. Abandoned following Ottoman conquest in the 16th century, Vardzia remains one of the most impressive medieval monuments in the entire Caucasus region.

activities.text: Spend several hours wandering through Vardzia's labyrinthine cave passages, discovering hidden chambers and appreciating the engineering ingenuity required to create this vertical city. The remote location surrounded by dramatic canyon landscapes adds to the site's mystical atmosphere. Active monks still maintain a small monastery within the complex, continuing traditions established nine centuries ago.

days_plan.itin_title: Caucasus Mountain Odyssey: A Road Trip to Medieval Towers & Ancient Cave Cities
